title: Acute Porphyria
oneLiner: Life-threatening neurovisceral attacks triggered by drugs/hormones; autosomal dominant, low penetrance, mimics surgical abdomen.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe, colicky abdominal pain that has been escalating over 2 days. She has nausea, vomiting, and constipation. She appears anxious and tachycardic (HR 110 bpm), BP 150/90 mmHg. She is afebrile. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness but no rigidity or guarding. She mentions her urine looks "dark" but not bloody. On questioning, she recently started oral contraceptives for acne. Previous similar episodes resolved spontaneously. She is of Northern European descent.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Young woman, 20s-30s | Acute porphyrias peak in reproductive age; hormonal triggers are common |
| Severe colicky abdominal pain with normal imaging | Pain out of proportion to exam — classic for visceral autonomic neuropathy |
| Nausea, vomiting, constipation | Autonomic dysfunction — gut dysmotility |
| Tachycardia, hypertension | Sympathetic overdrive secondary to neuronal damage |
| Anxiety, agitation | CNS involvement — porphyrin precursors neurotoxic |
| Dark urine (not bloody) | Porphobilinogen (PBG) in urine oxidises to porphyrins, giving port-wine colour |
| Recent OCP start | Estrogen/progesterone induce ALAS1, triggering attack |
| Previous similar episodes ± family history | Recurrent attacks; inheritance pattern (AD with low penetrance) |
| Afebrile, normal WCC | Differentiates from surgical abdomen (infection, inflammation) |

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Psychiatric mimic:** Acute psychosis, hallucinations, or severe anxiety without abdominal pain. Often misdiagnosed as schizophrenia or conversion disorder. Look for concurrent dysautonomia (tachycardia, hypertension).
- **Seizure mimic:** New-onset seizures in a young adult, especially if photosensitive (porphyrins are phototoxic). Can be mistaken for epilepsy. Check urine PBG after a seizure — it may be falsely negative if timed poorly.
- **Post-surgical complication:** Attack triggered by anaesthetic agents (barbiturates, etomidate) or starvation after surgery. Patient develops unexplained weakness, hyponatraemia, and dark urine post-operatively.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute porphyria refers to a group of inherited disorders of haem synthesis where defective enzyme activity leads to accumulation of toxic precursors (δ-aminolevulinic acid [ALA] and porphobilinogen [PBG]) that damage the autonomic, peripheral, and central nervous systems. The most common is **acute intermittent porphyria (AIP)**, followed by variegate porphyria (VP) and hereditary coproporphyria (HCP).

**Why this matters:**
- **Frequency:** Uncommon (~1 in 20,000 clinically penetrant), but easily missed. Up to 80% of gene carriers never have symptoms, so a negative family history does not exclude it.
- **Stakes:** Attacks are potentially fatal (respiratory paralysis, cardiac arrhythmia). Delayed treatment increases morbidity.
- **Common pitfall:** 90% of undiagnosed patients undergo at least one unnecessary laparotomy for "acute abdomen." Surgeons may find nothing abnormal, but the stress of surgery (starvation, anaesthesia) can worsen the attack.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The haem synthesis story:**  
Haem is produced in every cell, but 80% in bone marrow (for haemoglobin) and 15% in liver (for cytochromes P450). The rate-limiting step in liver is **ALAS1**, normally suppressed by haem (negative feedback). In acute porphyrias, a partial enzyme defect downstream causes accumulation of substrate — specifically ALA and PBG. When something induces ALAS1 (e.g., drugs, hormones, starvation), the defective enzyme cannot keep up, and precursors overflow.

**Why key signs happen:**

| Finding | Pathophysiologic mechanism |
|---------|----------------------------|
| Severe abdominal pain (autonomic neuropathy) | ALA is structurally similar to GABA; it inhibits GABA receptors in the gut, causing unopposed cholinergic activity → smooth muscle spasm, visceral hypersensitivity |
| Tachycardia, hypertension | ALA inhibits GABA-mediated inhibition of sympathetic outflow → central sympathetic overactivity |
| Peripheral motor neuropathy (weakness, areflexia) | Porphyrin precursors cause axonal degeneration, especially in large myelinated fibres; motor > sensory |
| Seizures, psychosis, hallucinations | ALA displaces GABA from receptors in the CNS → reduced seizure threshold; direct neurotoxicity |
| Hyponatraemia | SIADH from hypothalamic involvement + vomiting + salt wasting |
| Dark urine (port-wine, red) | PBG excreted in urine oxidises spontaneously to porphyrins (uroporphyrin, coproporphyrin) on exposure to light and air |
| Photosensitivity (only in VP and HCP) | Accumulated porphyrinogens in skin (not AIP) are phototoxic — react with UVA to form free radicals, causing blistering and fragility |

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The triad of **pain + autonomic overdrive + neuropsychiatric symptoms** in a young woman is the fingerprint. The dark urine confirms the overflow.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
All triggers work by inducing ALAS1 → increased demand on the deficient enzyme. Grouped by aetiology:

| Category | Trigger | Stem clue |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Drugs (most common iatrogenic trigger)** | Barbiturates, sulphonamides, rifampicin, metoclopramide, phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, ergot derivatives, alcohol | "Started phenytoin for seizures" or "Given metoclopramide for vomiting" |
| **Hormonal** | Progesterone (luteal phase, OCP), oestrogen, pregnancy | "Symptoms worse just before period" or "Started combined pill" |
| **Metabolic stress** | Fasting / starvation, low-carb diet, illness, surgery | "Didn't eat for 24 hours after dental work" or "Post-op day 2 with confusion" |
| **Infection** | Any acute infection (viral, bacterial) — febrile illness | "Fever and flu-like symptoms for 2 days" |
| **Alcohol** | Acute binge or chronic heavy drinking | "Drank heavily last night" |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable trigger in some attacks | "Attacks occur unpredictably" |

**Most common cause:** Drug-induced (especially barbiturates and sulphonamides) + hormonal triggers (luteal phase, OCP) are the top two. Starvation is a close third.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
The **European Porphyria Network (EPNET)** criteria require:
- Clinical suspicion (neurovisceral attack)
- Elevated urinary PBG (spot or 24h) — more than 5x upper limit of normal
- Confirmatory testing for specific enzyme defect

**Key tests table:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Spot urine PBG (qualitative, Watson-Schwartz or Hoesch test)** | Rapid bedside screen for PBG | Immediate result in ED | Positive = urgent treat; false positive with urobilinogen, methyldopa, phenazopyridine. **Confirm with quantitative.** |
| **24h urine PBG + ALA (quantitative)** | Levels of PBG and ALA | Confirms acute attack; monitors response | PBG > 10 mg/24h (normal <2) & ALA > 5 mg/24h (normal <1.5). Levels decline with treatment. |
| **Plasma porphyrin fluorescence scan** | Identifies porphyrin types | Differentiates AIP vs VP vs HCP | AIP: peak 621 nm; VP: peak 626 nm; HCP: peak 619 nm |
| **Faeces porphyrins** | Excess coproporphyrin III or protoporphyrin | Distinguishes VP/HCP from AIP | VP: increased faecal protoporphyrin; HCP: increased faecal coproporphyrin; AIP: normal faeces |
| **Erythrocyte PBG deaminase activity** | Measures enzyme level | Genetic confirmation (AIP) | Low activity in AIP (but may overlap with normal; ~10% false negative) |
| **DNA analysis (HMBS gene)** | Mutation in AIP | Counselling, family screening | Confirms diagnosis; useful for latent carriers |

**Single finding that confirms acute attack:** **Urinary PBG > 5x upper limit of normal** (spot quantitative > 10 mg/L). This is the gold standard. If normal, acute porphyria is essentially ruled out.

**Pattern recognition:** In the emergency department, **a positive Watson-Schwartz test** (urine turns red after adding Ehrlich's reagent + butanol extraction) should immediately trigger treatment — do not wait for quantitative results.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stop all offending drugs** (especially barbiturates, sulphonamides, metoclopramide).
- **Treat pain:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV (safe in porphyria — avoid pethidine and codeine).
- **Treat nausea:** Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV (safe) — avoid metoclopramide (contraindicated).
- **Correct hyponatraemia** if severe (<125 mmol/L): Slow correction with 3% hypertonic saline (0.5–1 mL/kg/h) only if symptomatic (seizures, GCS<15). Otherwise, withhold fluids — SIADH resolves with treatment.
- **If severe (tachycardia, hypertension, seizures, or established neuropathy):** **Haem arginate** 3 mg/kg IV over 15–30 minutes once daily for 3–4 days. This suppresses ALAS1 by providing exogenous haem. Available as Normosang (UK) or Panhematin (US). Must be reconstituted with human albumin, infused immediately. Monitor for anaphylaxis, phlebitis.

**Reasoning:** Haem arginate is the disease-modifying therapy — it restores negative feedback on ALAS1, halting precursor production. It’s the only way to stop progression of neuropathy. Without it, autonomic and motor damage may become irreversible.

**Alternative if haem arginate unavailable:** **High-dose glucose** (10% dextrose infusion at 200–300 g/day) — less potent but reduces ALAS1 via caloric suppression. Use as bridging therapy.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Monitor pain, BP, HR, neurological status** (strength, reflexes, sensation). Improvement in tachycardia and BP within 24h is a good sign.
- **Repeat urine PBG every 24h** — should decline by >50% within 3–4 days.
- **Nutrition:** Encourage oral feeding; if unable, give IV dextrose. Avoid prolonged fasting even after discharge.
- **Ventilatory support:** Monitor serial vital capacity (VC) or negative inspiratory force (NIF). If VC < 1 L or NIF < −20 cm H₂O, consider ICU for non-invasive or invasive ventilation. Respiratory failure is the leading cause of death.
- **Seizure management:** Avoid phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate. Use levetiracetam 500–1000 mg BID (safe) or IV magnesium for eclampsia mimic.

**Long-term:**
- **Avoidance:** Patient education — list of safe/unsafe drugs (downloadable card from European Porphyria Network). Avoid fasting, crash diets, alcohol.
- **Hormonal suppression:** For women with premenstrual attacks — GnRH analogue (e.g., leuprolide) or trial of oral contraceptives (but may worsen some — individual trial).
- **Liver transplantation** for severe, recurrent attacks (intractable disease) — removes the liver, the source of ALAS1 induction. Rare.
- **Genetic counselling:** Offer screening to first-degree relatives (DNA testing for known mutation).

**What NOT to do:**
- **DO NOT** give barbiturates (thiopental, phenobarbital) for sedation or seizure — they are the worst trigger.
- **DO NOT** use metoclopramide for nausea — contraindicated (induces ALAS1).
- **DO NOT** vigorously correct hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline unless symptomatic — risk of osmotic demyelination.
- **DO NOT** perform laparotomy — 90% will have a normal abdomen; unnecessarily worsens attack.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Acute Porphyria (AIP) | Surgical Abdomen (e.g., Appendicitis) | Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) | Acute Lead Poisoning |
|---------|-----------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, colicky, episodic, normal imaging | Localised, progressive, peritonitis | Recurrent, episodic, peritonitic with fever | Colicky, with constipation, ± peripheral neuropathy |
| **Exam** | Tachycardia, HTN, anxiety; no rebound/guarding | Rebound, guarding, rigidity | Rebound, guarding, fever | Abdominal tenderness; gingival lead line; wrist drop |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia | Usually normal | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | ↑ PBG/ALA in urine; normal WCC; hyponatraemia | ↑ WCC, neutrophils; lactate may be ↑; imaging positive | ↑ ESR, CRP; proteinuria (amyloid) | ↑ Lead (>10 μg/dL); basophilic stippling on smear; ↑ uric acid |
| **Key differentiator** | Urine PBG + dark urine; no fever; neurological symptoms | CT scan findings (appendicitis, diverticulitis) | Ethnicity (Mediterranean); fever + serositis; positive MEFV mutation | Lead exposure history; basophilic stippling; motor neuropathy (wrist/foot drop) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Respiratory paralysis** | Progressive weakness ascending from legs → arms → chest → diaphragm. Dyspnoea, shallow breathing, decreased SpO₂. | Vital capacity < 1 L; NIF < −20 cm H₂O; absent reflexes | Urgent ICU for non-invasive (BiPAP) or invasive ventilation. Start haem arginate immediately. |
| **Seizures** | Generalised tonic-clonic or focal status epilepticus. May be triggered by hyponatraemia. | GCS < 8; ongoing seizure activity; EEG shows epileptiform activity | IV levetiracetam 40–60 mg/kg over 15 min; correct hyponatraemia; start haem arginate. Avoid phenytoin. |
| **SIADH with severe hyponatraemia** | Confusion, headache, seizures, coma. | Na < 125 mmol/L; urine osmolality > 100; low plasma osmolality | Slow correction (<8 mmol/L/24h) with hypertonic saline if symptomatic; otherwise fluid restriction. Manage underlying attack. |
| **Psychiatric sequelae** | Acute psychosis, hallucinations, catatonia, or chronic depression. | Abnormal behaviour, paranoia, disorganised speech | Treat the attack with haem arginate; avoid antipsychotics (many induce ALAS1) — use haloperidol cautiously (low risk) or second-generation (olanzapine). |
| **Chronic kidney disease** | Repeated attacks → hypertension, proteinuria, renal failure. | ↑ creatinine, proteinuria; no haematuria | Manage hypertension; avoid nephrotoxic drugs; monitor GFR. |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma (long-term)** | Silent; screens may detect nodule. | ↑ AFP; liver mass on ultrasound | Annual ultrasound and AFP in all AIP patients from age 50. |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
- Patient presents in respiratory distress with flaccid quadriparesis areflexia — “acute ascending paralysis” similar to Guillain-Barré. But urine PBG is positive, and CSF protein is normal (no albuminocytologic dissociation).
- A young woman in ICU with status epilepticus, hyponatraemia, and dark urine: missed porphyria.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"AIP = Abdominal pain, Induced by drugs, Psychiatric symptoms, Porphyrinuria"**  
   - A: Abdominal pain (colicky)  
   - I: Induced by drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides)  
   - P: Psychiatric symptoms (anxiety, psychosis)  
   - P: Porphyrinuria (dark urine)

2. **"The 5 Ps of Acute Porphyria"**  
   - Pain (abdominal)  
   - Port-wine urine  
   - Polyneuropathy  
   - Psychiatric disturbance  
   - Pressure (hypertension, tachycardia)

3. **"Safe Drugs for Pain and Seizures"**  
   - Pain: Morphine (safe), avoid pethidine  
   - Seizure: Levetiracetam (safe), avoid phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
- **Dark urine?** Always ask about recent drug exposure (especially barbiturates, sulphonamides), OCP use, and fasting. Then do a Watson-Schwartz test.
- **Abdominal pain with normal CT + tachycardia + hypertension** = think porphyria until proven otherwise.
- **Hyponatraemia + seizures in a young woman** = consider SIADH from acute porphyria.
- **Refractory status epilepticus** in a patient on multiple AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine) may be an unrecognised porphyria attack made worse by those drugs.
- **A relative with "porphyria" or "blue/blood disease"** (archaic term) — ask specifically.
- **In the ED, do not give metoclopramide for vomiting in any young woman with abdominal pain** until porphyria is excluded.
- **The urine PBG test is only positive during an attack** — latent carriers have normal levels.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking dark urine for haematuria (urine dipstick negative for blood, but positive for porphyrins).
- Giving pethidine for pain (it can precipitate attack).
- Using phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis in a known porphyric — triggers more seizures.
- Discharging a patient with mild attack without counselling on trigger avoidance — patient returns with severe attack.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Criterion | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate > 24, SpO₂ < 94%, HR > 130) | Immediate review by medical registrar; consider ICU |
| **Vital capacity < 1 L** (or NIF < −20 cm H₂O) | Urgent senior review; prepare for intubation and ICU transfer |
| **Seizures** (any) | Call crash team if ongoing > 2 minutes; senior anaesthetist/neurologist |
| **GCS ≤ 13 or drop by ≥ 2** | Immediate review; CT head to rule out other causes; ICU if GCS < 9 |
| **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (non-hypotensive) | Reflects tissue hypoperfusion from sympathetic excess; consider ICU |
| **Symptomatic hyponatraemia** (seizures, GCS<15, Na < 120) | Call renal/ICU for hypertonic saline infusion; monitor Q2h Na |
| **Rapidly progressive weakness** (ascending, within hours) | Senior neurology review; ICU for ventilatory monitoring |
| **Unable to tolerate oral / IV haem arginate** (anaphylaxis, phlebitis) | Call pharmacy for alternative (high-dose dextrose); consider desensitisation |

**Thresholds for ICU admission:**  
- Any requirement for invasive monitoring (BP, CVP) due to severe dysautonomia  
- Ventilatory compromise (VC < 1 L or PaCO₂ > 50 mmHg)  
- Status epilepticus refractory to levetiracetam  
- Multiple organ dysfunction (AKI, hepatic failure) from severe attack

**Always have a low threshold to escalate** — porphyria attacks can accelerate over hours. Early senior involvement, haem arginate availability, and ICU support are the difference between recovery and catastrophe.

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Porphyria
**One-liner:** Life-threatening neurovisceral attacks triggered by drugs/hormones; autosomal dominant, low penetrance, mimics surgical abdomen.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe, colicky abdominal pain that has been escalating over 2 days. She has nausea, vomiting, and constipation. She appears anxious and tachycardic (HR 110 bpm), BP 150/90 mmHg. She is afebrile. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness but no rigidity or guarding. She mentions her urine looks "dark" but not bloody. On questioning, she recently started oral contraceptives for acne. Previous similar episodes resolved spontaneously. She is of Northern European descent.

**Dissect the vignette:**

| Pattern clue | What it tells you |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Young woman, 20s-30s | Acute porphyrias peak in reproductive age; hormonal triggers are common |
| Severe colicky abdominal pain with normal imaging | Pain out of proportion to exam — classic for visceral autonomic neuropathy |
| Nausea, vomiting, constipation | Autonomic dysfunction — gut dysmotility |
| Tachycardia, hypertension | Sympathetic overdrive secondary to neuronal damage |
| Anxiety, agitation | CNS involvement — porphyrin precursors neurotoxic |
| Dark urine (not bloody) | Porphobilinogen (PBG) in urine oxidises to porphyrins, giving port-wine colour |
| Recent OCP start | Estrogen/progesterone induce ALAS1, triggering attack |
| Previous similar episodes ± family history | Recurrent attacks; inheritance pattern (AD with low penetrance) |
| Afebrile, normal WCC | Differentiates from surgical abdomen (infection, inflammation) |

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Psychiatric mimic:** Acute psychosis, hallucinations, or severe anxiety without abdominal pain. Often misdiagnosed as schizophrenia or conversion disorder. Look for concurrent dysautonomia (tachycardia, hypertension).
- **Seizure mimic:** New-onset seizures in a young adult, especially if photosensitive (porphyrins are phototoxic). Can be mistaken for epilepsy. Check urine PBG after a seizure — it may be falsely negative if timed poorly.
- **Post-surgical complication:** Attack triggered by anaesthetic agents (barbiturates, etomidate) or starvation after surgery. Patient develops unexplained weakness, hyponatraemia, and dark urine post-operatively.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Acute porphyria refers to a group of inherited disorders of haem synthesis where defective enzyme activity leads to accumulation of toxic precursors (δ-aminolevulinic acid [ALA] and porphobilinogen [PBG]) that damage the autonomic, peripheral, and central nervous systems. The most common is **acute intermittent porphyria (AIP)**, followed by variegate porphyria (VP) and hereditary coproporphyria (HCP).

**Why this matters:**
- **Frequency:** Uncommon (~1 in 20,000 clinically penetrant), but easily missed. Up to 80% of gene carriers never have symptoms, so a negative family history does not exclude it.
- **Stakes:** Attacks are potentially fatal (respiratory paralysis, cardiac arrhythmia). Delayed treatment increases morbidity.
- **Common pitfall:** 90% of undiagnosed patients undergo at least one unnecessary laparotomy for "acute abdomen." Surgeons may find nothing abnormal, but the stress of surgery (starvation, anaesthesia) can worsen the attack.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The haem synthesis story:**  
Haem is produced in every cell, but 80% in bone marrow (for haemoglobin) and 15% in liver (for cytochromes P450). The rate-limiting step in liver is **ALAS1**, normally suppressed by haem (negative feedback). In acute porphyrias, a partial enzyme defect downstream causes accumulation of substrate — specifically ALA and PBG. When something induces ALAS1 (e.g., drugs, hormones, starvation), the defective enzyme cannot keep up, and precursors overflow.

**Why key signs happen:**

| Finding | Pathophysiologic mechanism |
|---------|----------------------------|
| Severe abdominal pain (autonomic neuropathy) | ALA is structurally similar to GABA; it inhibits GABA receptors in the gut, causing unopposed cholinergic activity → smooth muscle spasm, visceral hypersensitivity |
| Tachycardia, hypertension | ALA inhibits GABA-mediated inhibition of sympathetic outflow → central sympathetic overactivity |
| Peripheral motor neuropathy (weakness, areflexia) | Porphyrin precursors cause axonal degeneration, especially in large myelinated fibres; motor > sensory |
| Seizures, psychosis, hallucinations | ALA displaces GABA from receptors in the CNS → reduced seizure threshold; direct neurotoxicity |
| Hyponatraemia | SIADH from hypothalamic involvement + vomiting + salt wasting |
| Dark urine (port-wine, red) | PBG excreted in urine oxidises spontaneously to porphyrins (uroporphyrin, coproporphyrin) on exposure to light and air |
| Photosensitivity (only in VP and HCP) | Accumulated porphyrinogens in skin (not AIP) are phototoxic — react with UVA to form free radicals, causing blistering and fragility |

**Connect to pattern recognition:** The triad of **pain + autonomic overdrive + neuropsychiatric symptoms** in a young woman is the fingerprint. The dark urine confirms the overflow.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

All triggers work by inducing ALAS1 → increased demand on the deficient enzyme. Grouped by aetiology:

| Category | Trigger | Stem clue |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| **Drugs (most common iatrogenic trigger)** | Barbiturates, sulphonamides, rifampicin, metoclopramide, phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, ergot derivatives, alcohol | "Started phenytoin for seizures" or "Given metoclopramide for vomiting" |
| **Hormonal** | Progesterone (luteal phase, OCP), oestrogen, pregnancy | "Symptoms worse just before period" or "Started combined pill" |
| **Metabolic stress** | Fasting / starvation, low-carb diet, illness, surgery | "Didn't eat for 24 hours after dental work" or "Post-op day 2 with confusion" |
| **Infection** | Any acute infection (viral, bacterial) — febrile illness | "Fever and flu-like symptoms for 2 days" |
| **Alcohol** | Acute binge or chronic heavy drinking | "Drank heavily last night" |
| **Idiopathic** | No identifiable trigger in some attacks | "Attacks occur unpredictably" |

**Most common cause:** Drug-induced (especially barbiturates and sulphonamides) + hormonal triggers (luteal phase, OCP) are the top two. Starvation is a close third.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
The **European Porphyria Network (EPNET)** criteria require:
- Clinical suspicion (neurovisceral attack)
- Elevated urinary PBG (spot or 24h) — more than 5x upper limit of normal
- Confirmatory testing for specific enzyme defect

**Key tests table:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **Spot urine PBG (qualitative, Watson-Schwartz or Hoesch test)** | Rapid bedside screen for PBG | Immediate result in ED | Positive = urgent treat; false positive with urobilinogen, methyldopa, phenazopyridine. **Confirm with quantitative.** |
| **24h urine PBG + ALA (quantitative)** | Levels of PBG and ALA | Confirms acute attack; monitors response | PBG > 10 mg/24h (normal <2) & ALA > 5 mg/24h (normal <1.5). Levels decline with treatment. |
| **Plasma porphyrin fluorescence scan** | Identifies porphyrin types | Differentiates AIP vs VP vs HCP | AIP: peak 621 nm; VP: peak 626 nm; HCP: peak 619 nm |
| **Faeces porphyrins** | Excess coproporphyrin III or protoporphyrin | Distinguishes VP/HCP from AIP | VP: increased faecal protoporphyrin; HCP: increased faecal coproporphyrin; AIP: normal faeces |
| **Erythrocyte PBG deaminase activity** | Measures enzyme level | Genetic confirmation (AIP) | Low activity in AIP (but may overlap with normal; ~10% false negative) |
| **DNA analysis (HMBS gene)** | Mutation in AIP | Counselling, family screening | Confirms diagnosis; useful for latent carriers |

**Single finding that confirms acute attack:** **Urinary PBG > 5x upper limit of normal** (spot quantitative > 10 mg/L). This is the gold standard. If normal, acute porphyria is essentially ruled out.

**Pattern recognition:** In the emergency department, **a positive Watson-Schwartz test** (urine turns red after adding Ehrlich's reagent + butanol extraction) should immediately trigger treatment — do not wait for quantitative results.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stop all offending drugs** (especially barbiturates, sulphonamides, metoclopramide).
- **Treat pain:** Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV (safe in porphyria — avoid pethidine and codeine).
- **Treat nausea:** Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV (safe) — avoid metoclopramide (contraindicated).
- **Correct hyponatraemia** if severe (<125 mmol/L): Slow correction with 3% hypertonic saline (0.5–1 mL/kg/h) only if symptomatic (seizures, GCS<15). Otherwise, withhold fluids — SIADH resolves with treatment.
- **If severe (tachycardia, hypertension, seizures, or established neuropathy):** **Haem arginate** 3 mg/kg IV over 15–30 minutes once daily for 3–4 days. This suppresses ALAS1 by providing exogenous haem. Available as Normosang (UK) or Panhematin (US). Must be reconstituted with human albumin, infused immediately. Monitor for anaphylaxis, phlebitis.

**Reasoning:** Haem arginate is the disease-modifying therapy — it restores negative feedback on ALAS1, halting precursor production. It’s the only way to stop progression of neuropathy. Without it, autonomic and motor damage may become irreversible.

**Alternative if haem arginate unavailable:** **High-dose glucose** (10% dextrose infusion at 200–300 g/day) — less potent but reduces ALAS1 via caloric suppression. Use as bridging therapy.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Monitor pain, BP, HR, neurological status** (strength, reflexes, sensation). Improvement in tachycardia and BP within 24h is a good sign.
- **Repeat urine PBG every 24h** — should decline by >50% within 3–4 days.
- **Nutrition:** Encourage oral feeding; if unable, give IV dextrose. Avoid prolonged fasting even after discharge.
- **Ventilatory support:** Monitor serial vital capacity (VC) or negative inspiratory force (NIF). If VC < 1 L or NIF < −20 cm H₂O, consider ICU for non-invasive or invasive ventilation. Respiratory failure is the leading cause of death.
- **Seizure management:** Avoid phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate. Use levetiracetam 500–1000 mg BID (safe) or IV magnesium for eclampsia mimic.

**Long-term:**
- **Avoidance:** Patient education — list of safe/unsafe drugs (downloadable card from European Porphyria Network). Avoid fasting, crash diets, alcohol.
- **Hormonal suppression:** For women with premenstrual attacks — GnRH analogue (e.g., leuprolide) or trial of oral contraceptives (but may worsen some — individual trial).
- **Liver transplantation** for severe, recurrent attacks (intractable disease) — removes the liver, the source of ALAS1 induction. Rare.
- **Genetic counselling:** Offer screening to first-degree relatives (DNA testing for known mutation).

**What NOT to do:**
- **DO NOT** give barbiturates (thiopental, phenobarbital) for sedation or seizure — they are the worst trigger.
- **DO NOT** use metoclopramide for nausea — contraindicated (induces ALAS1).
- **DO NOT** vigorously correct hyponatraemia with hypertonic saline unless symptomatic — risk of osmotic demyelination.
- **DO NOT** perform laparotomy — 90% will have a normal abdomen; unnecessarily worsens attack.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Acute Porphyria (AIP) | Surgical Abdomen (e.g., Appendicitis) | Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF) | Acute Lead Poisoning |
|---------|-----------------------|---------------------------------------|------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, colicky, episodic, normal imaging | Localised, progressive, peritonitis | Recurrent, episodic, peritonitic with fever | Colicky, with constipation, ± peripheral neuropathy |
| **Exam** | Tachycardia, HTN, anxiety; no rebound/guarding | Rebound, guarding, rigidity | Rebound, guarding, fever | Abdominal tenderness; gingival lead line; wrist drop |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia | Sinus tachycardia | Usually normal | Usually normal |
| **Labs** | ↑ PBG/ALA in urine; normal WCC; hyponatraemia | ↑ WCC, neutrophils; lactate may be ↑; imaging positive | ↑ ESR, CRP; proteinuria (amyloid) | ↑ Lead (>10 μg/dL); basophilic stippling on smear; ↑ uric acid |
| **Key differentiator** | Urine PBG + dark urine; no fever; neurological symptoms | CT scan findings (appendicitis, diverticulitis) | Ethnicity (Mediterranean); fever + serositis; positive MEFV mutation | Lead exposure history; basophilic stippling; motor neuropathy (wrist/foot drop) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|-----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Respiratory paralysis** | Progressive weakness ascending from legs → arms → chest → diaphragm. Dyspnoea, shallow breathing, decreased SpO₂. | Vital capacity < 1 L; NIF < −20 cm H₂O; absent reflexes | Urgent ICU for non-invasive (BiPAP) or invasive ventilation. Start haem arginate immediately. |
| **Seizures** | Generalised tonic-clonic or focal status epilepticus. May be triggered by hyponatraemia. | GCS < 8; ongoing seizure activity; EEG shows epileptiform activity | IV levetiracetam 40–60 mg/kg over 15 min; correct hyponatraemia; start haem arginate. Avoid phenytoin. |
| **SIADH with severe hyponatraemia** | Confusion, headache, seizures, coma. | Na < 125 mmol/L; urine osmolality > 100; low plasma osmolality | Slow correction (<8 mmol/L/24h) with hypertonic saline if symptomatic; otherwise fluid restriction. Manage underlying attack. |
| **Psychiatric sequelae** | Acute psychosis, hallucinations, catatonia, or chronic depression. | Abnormal behaviour, paranoia, disorganised speech | Treat the attack with haem arginate; avoid antipsychotics (many induce ALAS1) — use haloperidol cautiously (low risk) or second-generation (olanzapine). |
| **Chronic kidney disease** | Repeated attacks → hypertension, proteinuria, renal failure. | ↑ creatinine, proteinuria; no haematuria | Manage hypertension; avoid nephrotoxic drugs; monitor GFR. |
| **Hepatocellular carcinoma (long-term)** | Silent; screens may detect nodule. | ↑ AFP; liver mass on ultrasound | Annual ultrasound and AFP in all AIP patients from age 50. |

**Late-presentation patterns:**  
- Patient presents in respiratory distress with flaccid quadriparesis areflexia — “acute ascending paralysis” similar to Guillain-Barré. But urine PBG is positive, and CSF protein is normal (no albuminocytologic dissociation).
- A young woman in ICU with status epilepticus, hyponatraemia, and dark urine: missed porphyria.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"AIP = Abdominal pain, Induced by drugs, Psychiatric symptoms, Porphyrinuria"**  
   - A: Abdominal pain (colicky)  
   - I: Induced by drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides)  
   - P: Psychiatric symptoms (anxiety, psychosis)  
   - P: Porphyrinuria (dark urine)

2. **"The 5 Ps of Acute Porphyria"**  
   - Pain (abdominal)  
   - Port-wine urine  
   - Polyneuropathy  
   - Psychiatric disturbance  
   - Pressure (hypertension, tachycardia)

3. **"Safe Drugs for Pain and Seizures"**  
   - Pain: Morphine (safe), avoid pethidine  
   - Seizure: Levetiracetam (safe), avoid phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate

**High-yield clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
- **Dark urine?** Always ask about recent drug exposure (especially barbiturates, sulphonamides), OCP use, and fasting. Then do a Watson-Schwartz test.
- **Abdominal pain with normal CT + tachycardia + hypertension** = think porphyria until proven otherwise.
- **Hyponatraemia + seizures in a young woman** = consider SIADH from acute porphyria.
- **Refractory status epilepticus** in a patient on multiple AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine) may be an unrecognised porphyria attack made worse by those drugs.
- **A relative with "porphyria" or "blue/blood disease"** (archaic term) — ask specifically.
- **In the ED, do not give metoclopramide for vomiting in any young woman with abdominal pain** until porphyria is excluded.
- **The urine PBG test is only positive during an attack** — latent carriers have normal levels.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking dark urine for haematuria (urine dipstick negative for blood, but positive for porphyrins).
- Giving pethidine for pain (it can precipitate attack).
- Using phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis in a known porphyric — triggers more seizures.
- Discharging a patient with mild attack without counselling on trigger avoidance — patient returns with severe attack.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**

| Criterion | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **NEWS2 ≥ 5** (especially respiratory rate > 24, SpO₂ < 94%, HR > 130) | Immediate review by medical registrar; consider ICU |
| **Vital capacity < 1 L** (or NIF < −20 cm H₂O) | Urgent senior review; prepare for intubation and ICU transfer |
| **Seizures** (any) | Call crash team if ongoing > 2 minutes; senior anaesthetist/neurologist |
| **GCS ≤ 13 or drop by ≥ 2** | Immediate review; CT head to rule out other causes; ICU if GCS < 9 |
| **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** (non-hypotensive) | Reflects tissue hypoperfusion from sympathetic excess; consider ICU |
| **Symptomatic hyponatraemia** (seizures, GCS<15, Na < 120) | Call renal/ICU for hypertonic saline infusion; monitor Q2h Na |
| **Rapidly progressive weakness** (ascending, within hours) | Senior neurology review; ICU for ventilatory monitoring |
| **Unable to tolerate oral / IV haem arginate** (anaphylaxis, phlebitis) | Call pharmacy for alternative (high-dose dextrose); consider desensitisation |

**Thresholds for ICU admission:**  
- Any requirement for invasive monitoring (BP, CVP) due to severe dysautonomia  
- Ventilatory compromise (VC < 1 L or PaCO₂ > 50 mmHg)  
- Status epilepticus refractory to levetiracetam  
- Multiple organ dysfunction (AKI, hepatic failure) from severe attack

**Always have a low threshold to escalate** — porphyria attacks can accelerate over hours. Early senior involvement, haem arginate availability, and ICU support are the difference between recovery and catastrophe.
